coredata.dc:title: Effect of heating rates on thermal runaway and gas composition in large Li-ion battery cell formats – An experimental study

coredata.dc:description: The growing use of high-energy lithium-ion batteries for energy storage raises fire safety concerns, especially under high charge/discharge rates or external heating. This work presents an experimental investigation of different heating rates into the thermal runaway behavior of two large cell formats, a 64 Ah NMC pouch cell and a 100 Ah LFP prismatic cell, focusing on key parameters such as surface temperature evolution, propagation time across cell surfaces, total mass loss, gas composition and volume, and total heat release. The tests were conducted in inert atmosphere in a pressurised vessel. Different heating rates, from 4 °C/min to 24 °C/min, and a test with an initial 24 °C/min increase followed by a plateau, were employed for fully charged cells. Results demonstrated for both cells that heating rates influenced the time to reach thermal runaway, the onset temperature, and the maximum temperatures, but had a minor impact on the total gas volume. The gas composition from LFP prismatic cells was found to correlate with the studied heating rates. The findings provide valuable empirical data that support the development of predictive models for thermal runaway initiation and heat propagation in battery modules, contributing to improved safety strategies and system-level design for energy storage applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing use of high-energy lithium-ion batteries for energy storage raises fire safety concerns, especially under high charge/discharge rates or external heating. This work presents an experimental investigation of different heating rates into the thermal runaway behavior of two large cell formats, a 64 Ah NMC pouch cell and a 100 Ah LFP prismatic cell, focusing on key parameters such as surface temperature evolution, propagation time across cell surfaces, total mass loss, gas composition and volume, and total heat release. The tests were conducted in inert atmosphere in a pressurised vessel. Different heating rates, from 4 °C/min to 24 °C/min, and a test with an initial 24 °C/min increase followed by a plateau, were employed for fully charged cells. Results demonstrated for both cells that heating rates influenced the time to reach thermal runaway, the onset temperature, and the maximum temperatures, but had a minor impact on the total gas volume. The gas composition from LFP prismatic cells was found to correlate with the studied heating rates. The findings provide valuable empirical data that support the development of predictive models for thermal runaway initiation and heat propagation in battery modules, contributing to improved safety strategies and system-level design for energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are rechargeable batteries commonly used as power sources for portable electronic devices, automobile and maritime applications, and stationary energy storage. In recent years, the application of large-format Li-ion cells for stationary energy storage applications has accelerated due to their higher energy density (Wh/L), enabled by greater active material content compared to smaller cells. Energy density also depends on the cell chemistry. For instance, pouch-type cells are projected to reach energy densities of up to 950 Wh/L by 2030, while prismatic-type cells may achieve up to 840 Wh/L using conventional liquid electrolyte technologies (Link et al., 2022). Alongside these advancements, the increasing cell size and energy density of large cells increases the potential danger of thermal runaway (TR), particularly due to the slower dissipation of heat generated during charge/discharge.

Thermal and health hazards are risks associated with Li-ion cells. Li-ion cells are subjected to failure when they are exposed to mechanical, electrical, or thermal abuse conditions (Wang et al., 2019a). Any of these abuses can damage the cell, leading to a cell internal short circuit (ISC). An ISC can occur when active cathode and anode materials are in contact during the decomposition and melting of the separator or if the separator is mechanically damaged. By thermal abuse such as external heating, the cell cannot dissipate the extra heat, leading to the production of gas inside the cell from the decomposition of the electrolyte and solid electrolyte interphase layer (SEI). Cell internal pressure starts rising until a critical value is reached, at which point the cell starts gas venting. Depending on cell format, the gas venting is achieved through the rupture of the flexible shell for pouch cell-types, while the opening of a safety valve for prismatic cell-types. As cell temperature increases, the internal cell components may interact, initiating exothermic reactions, and consequently contributing to the accumulation of heat (Wang et al., 2019a). TR initiates when the intense heat generation by exothermic reactions lead the cell to self-heating out of control. Here, hot flammable gases may be released and ignited at TR stage, leading to a possible flaming condition. A range of parameters impact TR behaviour, including cell parameters (active material in the cathode and anode, choice of electrolyte, the size and format, the state of charge (SoC) and the ageing) (Ribière et al., 2012; Ping et al., 2015; Fu et al., 2015; Lecocq et al., 2016; Larsson et al., 2018; Rong et al., 2019; Mao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024; Xie and Zhang, 2025), as well as experimental conditions (the boundary conditions of the test set up and the triggering method) (Willstrand et al., 2023).

Multiple studies have investigated the effect of different abuse triggers on TR initiation. A review of 76 publications by Rappsilber et al (Rappsilber et al., 2023) identified electrical heating, radiant heating, and fire heating as the most commonly used thermal methods. However, systematic comparisons remain challenging due to the wide variability in test conditions and rapid technological developments. Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2023) found that the influence of different trigger methods on TR behaviour can be as significant as that of varying SoC level. Parameters such as TR onset temperature, time to TR initiation, maximum temperature, mass loss, and gas release are all sensitive to the choice of trigger method and require further study—particularly in medium-sized cells (<100 Ah). Regarding effects of SoC, previous studies have reached a consensus that a high SoC results in more hazardous TR behaviour (Ribière et al., 2012; Ping et al., 2015; Fu et al., 2015; Lecocq et al., 2016; Rong et al., 2019; Mao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024; Xie and Zhang, 2025). SoC influences the TR onset temperature, total gas volume, gas production rate and gas composition (Willstrand et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). The TR behaviour at high SoC is characterised by higher maximum surface temperatures, elevated rates of heat release rate (HRR) and total heat generation, and greater mass loss (Ping et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Larsson et al., 2014; Golubkov et al., 2015). These findings underscore the importance of understanding how abuse conditions, particularly the heating method and SoC, contribute to TR behaviour, especially in large cell formats where the consequences of failure are magnified.

Among thermal abuse methods, electrical heating is widely used due to its controllability and relevance to practical scenarios. Parameters such as heating power, contact area, and heater positioning significantly influence the resulting TR behaviour (Wang et al., 2019b; Jin et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2022; Jin et al., 2022). Despite the relevance of these parameters, few studies have examined the influence of heating rate (°C/min) on TR behaviour for different cell chemistries and medium to large cell formats, including NMC pouch cell (Willstrand et al., 2023; Gagnon et al., 2023), NMC prismatic cell (Huang et al., 2022), LCO pouch cell (Kurzawski et al., 2023), NMC cylindrical cell (Goupil et al., 2022), and LFP pouch cell (Peng et al., 2020). The impact of heat generation from a heater, for example on maximum temperature, was investigated for fully charged medium and large NMC cells with capacities of 25 Ah (Jin et al., 2022), 63 Ah (Gagnon et al., 2023), 94 Ah (Huang et al., 2022), 157 Ah (Willstrand et al., 2023), as well as for 60 Ah LFP cells (Peng et al., 2020). For instance, Huang et al (Huang et al., 2022) studied four prismatic NMC 94 Ah cells in parallel at different heating power (400–700 W), and the maximum average temperature increases from 634.7 °C to 659.6 °C. Gagnon et al (Gagnon et al., 2023) found an equal peak surface temperature of 756 ± 34 °C for NMC 63 Ah pouch cells at three heating rates (0.2 °C/sec to 0.5 °C/sec). Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2023) observed peak temperatures of a prismatic 157 Ah NMC cell at 1 °C/min and 20 °C/min rates of 612–614 °C to 716–786 °C. In addition, Peng et al (Peng et al., 2020) observed a peak temperature of 578.3 °C, 566.8 °C and 528.6 °C in a 60 Ah LFP cell at three different incident heat fluxes (kW/m2) (6.5, 8.8 and 11.1 kW/m2). These findings suggest that heating rate influences TR behaviour (E.g. time to TR initiation, TR onset temperature, maximum temperatures and more), especially the propagation time across cell surfaces, which in turn affects the potential for heat transfer to adjacent cells in a module configuration. The heating rates (4–24 ℃/min) in this study were chosen to cover a representative range of thermal abuse scenarios, such as a possible thermal propagation from neighbouring cells or exposure to external fire sources, and for comparison of results with previous studies. In addition, most literature on electrical heating focuses on uniform heat conduction—either applied across one or both sides of the cell—or local heating via cylindrical heaters. To the best of the authors' knowledge, the effect of non-uniform local heating rates on TR behaviour and propagation time has not been thoroughly investigated for either NMC or LFP chemistries in large pouch or prismatic cell formats. This is a critical knowledge gap, as localized heating reflects more realistic abuse conditions and poses significant safety challenges in battery module configurations.

This study aims to address this gap by experimentally investigating the effect of different local heating rates on TR behaviour in large cell formats using a pressurised vessel under inert atmosphere. The study focuses on 64 Ah NMC pouch cells and 100 Ah LFP prismatic cells, evaluating TR onset temperature, propagation time, total mass loss, gas composition and volume, and total heat release.

The novelty of this work lies in its systematic exploration of non-uniform local heating rates and their impact on TR behaviour and gas composition in large-capacity cells. A resistive local heater is employed to simulate realistic abuse conditions, enabling a more representative assessment of safety risks in energy storage systems.

The findings provide valuable empirical data to inform and calibrate computational models for predicting TR initiation and propagation, thereby improving thermal abuse testing methodologies. These insights are particularly relevant for enhancing the safety of stationary battery energy storage systems and support professionals involved in cell design, TR propagation mitigation, and fire risk analysis.

A total of 7 tests were conducted on two types of large-format Li-ion cells: pouch and prismatic. The experimental procedure of the tested methodology is illustrated in Fig. 1, and it consists of four main steps: (1) set-up preparation, (2) test execution, (3) gas sampling, and (4) data analysis. During the set-up preparation, some thermocouples are assembled into cell types, and some are placed inside the pressure vessel (See Section 2.1.2). A resistive local heater is placed on a side of the cell surface. The heating is controlled by a control heating element. The vessel is completely closed once heating is initiated, the occurrence of TR process is monitored by cell surface temperature. Heating is stopped either after TR occurs or when the cell surface temperature cools down, and subsequently, data is stopped. The gas accumulated within the vessel is simultaneously sampled using an air bag and measured to determine the total gas volume. The gas sample is then analysed using gas chromatography (GC). Finally, all data is post-processed.

Two cell types were used in the current study, their geometry, energy storage capacity, dimensions and SoC are listed in Table 1. Fig. 2 shows the dimensions of a 64 Ah pouch cell with NMC as the cathode, graphite as the anode, and a proprietary liquid electrolyte. The NMC cell has a voltage of 4.2 V at 100 % SoC and masses between 1156 g and 1170 g. Fig. 3 shows the dimensions of a 100 Ah prismatic cell with an LFP cathode. The LFP cell has a voltage of 3.65 V at 100 % SoC and masses are between 2120 g and 2128 g. Both cells were new large-format cells. The pouch cell is designed with a flexible casing, while the prismatic cell is designed with a hard casing and a vent port.

During the sample preparation, the cells were charged as recommended by the manufacturer, using a Chroma 62050P-100–100 3.3 kW Programmable DC Power Supply 100 VDC/100 A/5000 W. The NMC pouch cell was charged to the terminal voltage of 4.2 V through a constant-current and constant-voltage method (with a charge current of 32 A, with a cut-off current of 3.2 A). For the LFP prismatic cell, the cell was charged to the terminal voltage of 3.65 V through constant-current and constant-voltage method (with a charge current of 32 A, with a cut-off current of 3.2 A). All cells were charged to 100 % SoC before the start of the test. All cells were tested between 1 h and 8 h after the cell charging was finished. The cells were weighed before and after the test in a modular balance platform (Cubis MCM60K2 manual mass).

Table 2 illustrates the chemistry and heating rates for each test series. The heating rates were estimated to range from 4 °C/min to 24 °C/min, which is in line with previous TR behaviour and TR propagation studies using large cell formats (Goupil et al., 2022; Gagnon et al., 2023; Willstrand et al., 2023). Slow heating rates were used to achieve a more uniform temperature distribution, which can assume cell separator temperature closer to cell surface temperature. Heating rates for pouch-type cells were 4 °C/min and 12 °C/min, while heating rates for prismatic-type cells were 12 °C/min, 24 °C/min and a variable heating profile of initial 24 °C/min increase for 10 min followed by a temperature plateau at 250 °C. The decision-making process for selecting heating rates for prismatic cells is detailed in the results section.

A local copper plate heater was used as the heat source to initiate TR in the cells. The heater, with rectangular dimensions of 15.2 cm by 7.6 cm, operates at a voltage of 230 V and has a power output of 700 W. Two 0.5 mm type-K thermocouple (TC) probes were mounted to monitor the cell heating rate. One TC was positioned between the copper plate and the cell to monitor the temperature increase by the heater. Another TC was connected to the control heating element (10 A, 240 V). The location of the heater was on the side of the negative terminal for pouch-type cells and on the side of cell terminals for prismatic-type cells, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3. The heating of the cell surface was local and not uniform, causing temperature gradients, and the propagation of heat through the cell in the lower cell surface (side 2) and the upper cell surface (side 1) can be observed and studied.

TCs of type K 30 AWG-gauge (with a range temperature of −270 °C to 1370 °C) were placed on the upper and lower cell surfaces. On side 1 of the cell, the cell surface was in contact with the heater; four thermocouples (TCs 2, 3, 4 and 5) measured temperatures in the centre of the cell at different distances (see Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). TC7 was placed between the heater and the cell surface. Note that two thermocouples were placed at the TC7 location, one thermocouple was used as feedback to the heater control unit but not logged, while the other, labelled TC7, was only used for logging the temperature. On side 2, the opposite cell surface, three TCs were placed (TC17, TC18, TC19).

The sample fixture used in the study consists of the copper plate heater, the cell and two custom steel plates to hold the entire set-up together. The pattern without solid fill shown in the figures was steel material. Pouch cells expand and deform during the abuse test, therefore, one of the steel plates was designed with spring loading to control and even out the suspension force. A silicone paste (P12, heat conducting paste 0.8 W/mK) was used to add TCs close to the heater, additionally a fiberglass insulation layer of 0.32 cm thickness was placed at the top of the heater. According to literature on venting behaviour of pouch cells, the set-up was stacked horizontally. The sample fixture was modified for LFP prismatic cells, where two custom steel plates hold the entire set-up without spring loading. The cells were installed into the sample fixture, as shown in Figs. 2 and 3 and placed upon the load cell.

A ground wire was connected to the heater to prevent interference with TC measurements from the power supply to the copper plate heater. After the peak temperature was reached during the TR stage, the heater was switched off, and the cell was allowed to cool by 100 – 200 ◦C, after which data acquisition was stopped.

The cells were tested in an 80 litre pressure vessel consisting rated for up to 32 bar at 200 °C filled with an inert atmosphere ( Fig. 4), which in this case was nitrogen gas. Once closed, the vessel was flushed with a steady supply of nitrogen, resulting in an inert environment with < 1 % oxygen. Any gases released by the cell during testing accumulated inside the vessel. The pressure inside the vessel was monitored by two pressure sensors calibrated for 0 – 4 bar and 0 – 25 bar, respectively. Two additional TCs inside the pressure vessel measured gas temperatures at the upper and lower layers. The set-up was designed by the fire research group at RISE. The closed tests provide TR onset temperature, Tonset TR, and the pressure increase during TR. Tonset TR is the temperature as the cell start an uncontrollable exothermic reaction and accelerate its self-heating. The pressure, temperature and moles in the vessel can be converted by the ideal gas law to the total gas release volume. The total gas volume was also measured by a flow gas meter Metrixitalia M231450. The estimation of these parameters is according to the method by Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2023). The main advantage of the pressure vessel is that there is no reaction of the released gases with the inert atmosphere.

After TR, gases were extracted from the vessel and filled into gas bags. The gas composition of permanent gases and hydrocarbons in the bags was analysed with an Agilent 490 Micro-GC gas analyser. The instrument was calibrated using a synthetic mixture of ambient gases O2 and N2. The uncertainty was about 2 % of absolute values.
At the end of the tests, the remaining gas was evacuated from the pressure vessel, which was then cleaned. The remaining of the cells were weighed after tests for the measurement of total mass loss.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method to determine total gas volume
ce:section.ce:para: The total gas volume was estimated by two different methods. The first was a direct measurement of gas volume using a gas flow meter apparatus. The second method was by the ideal gas law considering temperature and pressure measurements within the vessel (Eq. 1, Eq. 2 and Eq. 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method to determine total heat released within pressure vessel
ce:section.ce:para:
Total heat release (THR) in kJ from cell gassing in a pressure vessel was estimated based on the dynamic pressure buildup as a result of temperature and pressure changes in a constant volume of an ideal gas (Karlsson and Quintiere, 2022). In an inert atmosphere, any O2 available for combustion originates from exothermic reactions within the cell. In such circumstances, the pressure within the vessel raises rapidly during cell TR. The conservation of energy and the continuity equation for mass are applicable to a system or to a control volume (CV). In this case, the CV was considered the pressure vessel and the introduction of mass into the CV was represented by the venting of battery gases (
m
̇
j) (see Fig. 5). The equation states that the rate of change in internal energy of a CV and the net rate of energy into the CV are equal to the net rate of heat added to the CV minus the rate of work done by the fluid in the CV. At constant volume, no work is performed, and all added heat increases the system's internal energy. The heat transfer rate to the CV(
Q
̇
) is derived from the differential equations dU = Cv⋅dT and dH = Cp⋅dT.

The heat output rate (
Q ,

kW) is described in Eq. 4, assuming a closed system with negligible fluid flow into the system. The total heat release
( Q , kJ )
is given in Eq. 5. Some assumptions were made in the model: the energy release rate is constant, the mass loss rate of the fuel is neglected in the mass conservation equation, specific heat capacities are assumed to not change with temperature, the hydrostatic pressure difference due to vessel height is considered negligible compared to the dynamic pressure, as stated in (Karlsson and Quintiere, 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell temperature behaviour during thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para:
This section presents the temperature development during TR for the investigated pouch and prismatic cells. The analysis focuses on the four stages of a TR, as noted (Jia et al., 2022):
a)
the cell preheating, during which the temperature increase only depends on the imposed heat flux and the heat conduction within the cell,
b)
the heat generation, during which the exothermal decomposition reactions contribute to the temperature increase,
c)
the exponential temperature increase (TR stage), up until the maximum TR temperature is reached, and
d)
the cooling stage.

However, no differentiation between the first two stages, cell preheating and heat generation, is made due to the chosen heating method that forces a constant heating rate. This means that external heating is automatically reduced once internal heat generation contributes to the temperature increase.

The temperature profile of the heater (TC7) and the cell surface temperatures, obtained in Test 2 and Test 4 of pouch cells, are shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 7 shows the same data type but for Test 5, Test 6 and Test 7 of the prismatic cells. The temperature trends observed in the pouch cell tests were more consistent with each other than the temperature development observed during the prismatic cell tests. Fig. 7 includes the temperature measurement for all prismatic cell tests. Based on the obtained temperature profiles, TR characteristics were obtained, such as the time until TR, the cell temperature at the start of TR (TR onset temperature) and the maximum TR temperature. These results are summarised in Table 3. The TR onset time was defined as the point at which the measured temperature first exceeded the specified temperature gradient threshold of 1.67 °C/s.

The temperature measurements for the prismatic cell tests showed unexpected behaviour. In both Test 5 (12 °C/min) and Test 6 (24 °C/min), the heater temperature (TC7) plateaued around 300 °C to 350 °C before TR was eventually triggered (see Fig. 7a and Fig. 7b). Fig. 7b illustrates a drop in the heating rate due to an interruption in the power supply from the heater control unit, which was unable to supply heat during this period. In Test 7 prismatic, i.e., the test with variable heating profile (an initial 24 °C/min heating rate for 10 min, followed by a temperature plateau at 250 ℃), TC7 did not experience a constant temperature rise and did not reach 250 °C before TR (See Fig. 7c). However, the thermocouple used for feedback in the heater control unit indicated that the target temperature of 250 °C was reached. The discrepancies with TC7 suggested that the TR initiation should be determined using thermocouples placed at the prismatic cell terminals. This assumption is justified by the fact that the tab temperature may better reflect the TR onset temperature than the surface temperature, as the cell casing has a large impact on the heat transfer, as noted in (Hunt et al., 2016). In addition, the supplementary data includes measurements of terminal temperatures, pressure buildup within the vessel and voltage drop in both pouch and prismatic cells.

The heating rate’s impact on the TR onset time and temperatures for pouch and prismatic cells was examined. Table 2 reveals that faster heating rates lead to shorter time before TR initiates and lower TR onset temperatures in pouch cells. However, the relationship between TR onset temperature and heating rate is less clear for prismatic cells, as shown in Fig. 7, which is discussed later in this section. The results of pouch cell tests demonstrate minor variations in TR onset temperature and time to TR for the same heating rate. Some factors in the experimental set-up, such as a variation of the thermal contact between the heater and the cell, and the steel plates and the cell, can cause these variations. In the case of prismatic cells, each test was conducted with different heating rates. Test 6 prismatic exhibited a comparable TR time to Test 5 prismatic. Whereas Test 5 prismatic exhibited a higher TR onset temperature in comparison to Test 6 prismatic. This discrepancy is mainly attributed to the variable heating profile of Test 6 prismatic in comparison to Test 5 prismatic, which allows a more uniform heating profile across the entire cell.

In addition, the discrepancies of time to TR and TR onset temperature for the same heating rate may also partly be attributed to variations in the composition of the SEI layer at the anode. The operating temperature of the cell impacts the reaction kinetics of the electrolytes on the electrode surface, generating different components of the SEI layer (Dong et al., 2022). According to literature, battery thermal instability is influenced by the mass ratio of cathode and electrolyte content. The increase of electrolyte content in the mixture of electrolyte and cathode may generate a greater number of exothermic peaks and greater total heat generation (Wang et al., 2011). However, it is assumed that the electrolyte composition and additives are similar in each tested cell type. Another important parameter is the cell heat capacity (combined heat capacities of electrode materials, electrolyte, separator and cell casing), which directly influences the time required to heat the cell. A meta-analysis by Steinhardt et al (Steinhardt et al., 2022) provided specific heat capacity values for full LIBs. These values range from approximately 800 to 1400 J/kgK, and denoted high-energy cells typically having lower heat capacities. In general, LFP prismatic cells have higher heat capacity than the NMC pouch cells, but there is no trend between pouch and prismatic cells for the same type of chemistry (Steinhardt et al., 2022). The differences in heat capacities among the pouch cells tested in this study may also explain the variations in time to TR and TR onset temperatures for the same heating rate. Besides, the onset temperature of exothermic reactions in NMC cells is influenced by the nickel content in the cathode. A higher Ni content will also lower the TR onset temperature and increase the amount of oxygen released (Wang et al., 2019a). However, this factor is unlikely to have significantly impacted the results of the pouch cells. The variability of electrolyte composition, formation of SEI layer, and heat capacity among identical cells are additional critical factors that influence TR behaviour in our study.

The maximum temperature was observed by TC7 on Side 1 of the cells. Peak temperatures for the NMC pouch cells were 550–710 °C, while peak temperatures for the LFP prismatic cells are 380–500 °C (see Table 3). In our experiments, NMC pouch cells at heating rates of 4 °C/min reached maximum temperatures of 662 °C and 708 °C (46 °C difference), whereas at 12 °C/min, the maximum temperatures were 680 °C and 551 °C (129 °C difference). The large temperature variation observed in the NMC cells for the same heating rate makes it difficult to establish a clear relationship with heating rate. A comparison of the preceding articles (Willstrand et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2022; Jin et al., 2022; Gagnon et al., 2023; Kurzawski et al., 2023; Goupil et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2020) reveals that some show no dependency with heating rates, whereas others do. The test results from Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2023)., who studied a 157 Ah NMC prismatic cell in a pressure vessel similar to our test set-up, demonstrated a significant variation in maximum temperature for the same heating rate with increasing heating rates (from 1 °C/min to 20 °C/min). Specifically, the temperature difference ranges from 2 °C for a heating rate of 1 °C/min to 79 °C for a rate of 20 °C/min, with large temperature variation observed at higher heating rates. Overall, the maximum temperature increased with higher heating rates. However, the increased heating rate exacerbated the uncertainty in the maximum temperature values, possibly due to the variability of gas venting rate from the cell. The variation in maximum temperature for the tested NMC pouch cells at similar heating rates was greater compared to the variation in TR onset temperatures, which is consistent with the findings of Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2023). Additional test repetitions in pouch cells are needed to gain a deeper understanding of the relationship between peak temperatures and heating rates. For the tested LFP prismatic cells, a single test per heating rate does not clearly establish this relationship. However, Peng et al (Peng et al., 2020). observed a decrease in peak temperature with increasing heating rates, suggesting a potential relationship that requires further investigation.

The cell casing design and the pressure relief mechanism are key factors influencing TR (Zhu et al., 2023). Zhu et al. (2023) examined the thermal behaviour of cells with the same cell chemistry (NMC), cell capacity (25 Ah) and heating procedure, but different cell formats (pouch and prismatic). Their findings indicated that NMC prismatic cells required a longer time to reach TR than NMC pouch cells. The cell chemistry, cell capacity and cell format presented in this paper are distinct from those described by Zhu et. al, and therefore not comparable. Nevertheless, the time required to reach TR is influenced by the internal cell pressure threshold at which gas is released, as well as the rate of gas venting during the TR process. TR variability at the same heating rate is more pronounced in pouch cells due to their flexible casing and inconsistent venting behaviour. Prismatic cells have structured venting mechanisms, leading to a more controlled TR response.

Continuing the comparison between both cell types, it is important to highlight that the chemistry and the stored energy density influence the maximum temperature of cells exposed to heat (Lamb et al., 2021). The tested NMC cells exhibited higher maximum temperatures compared to the tested LFP cells (see Table 3). Previous research observed that NMC cells reach higher maximum temperatures than LFP cells due to oxygen release, which sustains exothermic reactions and increases heat generation. In contrast, some studies suggest that LFP does not release oxygen at high temperatures (Wang et al., 2019a). In this study, the inert atmosphere within the pressure vessel controlled the initial oxygen concentration (below 1 %), ensuring that the vented gases did not undergo combustion. While this setup accurately represents LFP cells, it does not fully apply to NMC cells, as oxygen is released when the crystalline structure of NMC materials decomposes. Clearly, as previously stated, chemistry and stored energy are additional influencing factors in TR behaviour, alongside the other factors discussed earlier.

Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 show also the temperature envelope, i.e., the region in which all measured surface temperatures fall. Two different regions are distinguished: side 1, the side of the heater and side 2. It should be noted that TC7, the heater temperature, was not included in this region as it has a significantly larger temperature gradient than all the other thermocouples.

In the case of pouch cells, the preheating phase exhibited minor discrepancies in temperature difference between each cell surface (Overlap of side 1 and side 2, see Figs. 6a and 6c). The casing of the cell impacts heat conduction during the heating phase. After TR, side 1 post-TR temperatures (TC2, TC3, TC4 and TC5) were lower than post-TR temperatures on side 2 (TC16, TC17 and TC18); this means that there is a great temperature difference between planes within the cell. The peak temperature was observed on both sides of the cell at the same interval of time. After the peak temperature, side 1 presented lower temperatures compared to side 2. Side 2 temperature increased while Side 1 temperature cooled down. The temperature increases in side 2 means that a driving force of dT within cell components causes the temperature increase. A comparable analysis was conducted on prismatic cells, for which the preheating phase exhibited a notable temperature difference between each cell surface (see Figs. 7a, 7b and 7c). In TR, the peak temperature was slightly higher for side 1 than for side 2. After TR, the temperature cooled down in both sides. After TR, the temperature envelopes on side 1 and side 2 were consistent in all prismatic cell tests. The thermal conductivity across the cell (perpendicular to the electrode) is generally lower than the thermal conductivity along the cell (parallel to the electrodes) by one to two orders of magnitude (Wei et al., 2020). The difference in thermal conductivity combined with the non-uniform heating of the cell explain the temperature difference on side 1 and on side 2 described above.

One of the battery cells’ TR characteristics is the TR propagation within the cell, i.e., the delay between TR detection on side 1 and TR detection on side 2. The approximate duration for TR to propagate along side 1, across the cell, and along side 2 can be estimated based on the temperature derivative (dT/dt). Fig. 8 shows the temperature derivative for different heating rates in all pouch cell tests. The propagation time was estimated by the time delay between the temperature derivative peaks on different locations, i.e. TC2 to TC5 for the propagation along side 1, TC2 to TC17 and, TC4 to TC18 for the propagation across the cells thickness and TC16 to TC18 propagation for the propagation along side 2. The resulting times are summarised in Table 4.

For pouch cells, the TR propagation time at a heating rate of 4 °C/min (Test 2 pouch cell) was negligible along side 1, i.e., the time delay for the different TCs does not correlate to their distance to the heater and, hence, the location of the initial TR. The propagation time from side 1 to side 2 was 15 sec, and along side 2 was 3 sec. A similar pattern was observed for Test 1 pouch cell. In contrast, at a heating rate of 12 °C/min (Test 4 pouch cell), the time along side 1 was 3 sec, the time for side 1 to side 2 was 8.4 sec and the time along side 2 was 4.8 sec. Interestingly, Test 3 pouch cell does not exhibit similar results to Test 4; the propagation time from side 1 to side 2 was closer to the results at rates of 4 °C/min. Nevertheless, the measurements obtained for tests at 12 °C/min were supported by the work of Gagnon et al., who studied a similar battery pouch cell of 63 Ah (Gagnon et al., 2023) at the same heating rate (i.e. 12 °C/min), reporting a TR propagation time of 14 ± 1 sec through the cell surfaces (cell thickness), comparable to Test 3 in this study. Gagnon et al. further investigated faster heating rates (18 °C/min and 24 °C/min) and highlighted that the TR propagation time across the cell thickness increased with increasing heating rates. However, the present study was not able to confirm this observation. Slower heating rates led to faster propagation along side 1, which can be explained by the more uniform preheating at slower heating rates. This effect of heating rates on the temperature distribution in the cell before TR is illustrated in Fig. 6. The effect was strongest on side 1 but could not clearly be seen across the cell or on side 2, which is attributed to the higher thermal conductivity in planar direction.

The measurements obtained on the prismatic cell surfaces did not lead to reliable data for the assessment of TR propagation times within the cell, as the data was much noisier compared to the pouch cell data. Therefore, no figures for the prismatic cells are included in the present section. Indeed, according to the authors’ knowledge, limited literature is available regarding TR propagation time for a prismatic cell exposed to different heating rates. Huang et al (Huang et al., 2022). observed four prismatic cells with a total of 94 Ah interconnected in parallel that the propagation time into each cell increase with the heating power. For a single LFP cell a delayed TR propagation time across the cell is observed from 7 sec to 17 sec as heating power increases (Huang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total mass loss and cell damage after TR
ce:section.ce:para: The total mass loss of the cells was examined by measuring the cells before and after the abuse tests. The pouch cells were completely disintegrated and scattered throughout the vessel at the end of the test, presenting a challenge to the measurement of weight. Therefore, the mass loss for pouch cells was not measured. In comparison, Gagnon et al (Gagnon et al., 2023). observed in similar NMC pouch cells of 1177 g an average percent of initial mass loss of 40 % for the heating rates 12 ℃/min and 18 ℃/min, with a maximum fluctuation of 3 % across all heating rates. Gagnon et al. also employed a heating rate of 24 ℃/min, which resulted in an initial mass loss of 38 %. Minor variations in total mass loss were attributed to differences in the internal decomposition of components and gas venting. However, the relationship between total mass loss and heating rates in pouch cells remains unclear. To the authors’ knowledge, this relationship has not been investigated for heating rates higher than 24℃/min.

The total mass loss for prismatic cells is shown in Table 5. No notable differences were observed in the test outcomes at different heating rates. Increasing the heating rate from 12 to 24 °C/min resulted in a 1 % increase in total mass loss, which is within the margin of experimental uncertainty. These results indicate that, for the prismatic cells tested, total mass loss is not dependent on the applied heating rate.
The total gas composition for both cell-types and different heating rates are shown, respectively, in Table 6 and Table 7.

Fig. 9 shows, the gas temperatures within the pressure vessel (Fig. 9a.), the volume of gas vented from the cell (Vcell vent) and the pressure increase within the vessel (Fig. 9b and Fig. 9c) for Test 3 NMC pouch cell. Vcell vent has a curve that became distorted due to changes in temperature distribution inside the vessel during the TR stage. The pressure increased as the cell released gases and goes into TR. After TR, the Vcell vent reached a state of equilibrium and a steady state value, until it starts to first slightly decrease, followed by a more rapid decrease around 35 min. The rapid decrease was related to the filling of the gas bag for posterior gas analysis.

Table 8 and Table 9 present the total gas volume estimated by a flow meter apparatus (Flow Gas Meter [L]), and the pressure and temperature within the vessel (Vcell Vessel T [L]) for each cell test. The total gas volume estimated by Flow Gas Meter [L] shows comparable results to the total gas volume estimated by the Vcell Vessel T [L] (Eq. 3) for both cell-types (see Table 8 and Table 9). The accumulated gas volume per test obtained by the flow gas meter apparatus is normalised by the Ah per cell. The accumulated gas volume normalised by Ah increased slightly (approx. 0.1 L/Ah) as the heating rate incrased. The supplementary documentation provides the correlation between Vcell Vessel T [L] and Flow Gas Meter [L], along with a 95 % of confidence interval. This indicates that either method provides a reliable estimation of the total gas volume released by the battery cells.

The samples of gas species from the chamber were analysed at the end of each test by micro-GC analysis. The species identified were hydrogen, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, oxygen and a combination of some hydrocarbons, such as methane, ethene, ethylene, and propylene. During the later stages, certain species may be lost due to the initial outgassing of the cell. These lost gases likely include electrolyte decomposition products, such as fluorine and phosphorus components from lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), and evaporation of H2O.

The decomposition of cell components at elevated temperatures and the subsequent formation of gas species during exothermic reactions on the anode have been extensively discussed in the literature (Wang et al., 2019a), and are not the focus of this study. However, the percentage of gases released from NMC and LFP cells differs due to several complex and interrelated factors (Bugryniec et al., 2024), including the thermal decomposition of the cathode material, the electrolyte composition, the cell structural design (pouch vs prismatic forms), cell capacity and experimental conditions such as the heating rate applied to the cell and the surrounding atmosphere (inert vs. open). For instance, the selection of thicker or thinner electrodes within the active material affects the electrolyte content (Zheng et al., 2012), which in turn influences temperature gradients, as well as the composition and the amount of off-gases produced (Rong et al., 2023). A deep understanding of these root causes and the intricate interactions between battery factors during TR remains highly challenging. For this reason, the comparison between the two tested cells has its limitations and is primarily based on the conclusions drawn from existing literature (Bugryniec et al., 2024). Key observations made and compared with the experiments include:
•
NMC cells produce more off-gas than LFP cells. LFP have greater H2 content while NMC have greater CO content.
•
The composition of off-gas on average is very similar between NMC and LFP cells.
•
Prismatic cells tend to generate larger off-gas volumes than pouch cells.

The results showed that gas composition in NMC cells is consistent regardless of heating rates. The production of H2 slightly increases with faster heating rates, while the production of CO and CO2 remained similar with heating rates (see Table 6). In contrast, in the case of a 50 Ah NMC cell studied by Wang et al. (2019b) during open testing, it was concluded that CO2 gas production increased with the rising heating power, while the production of CO decreased. In open tests, the combustion tends to be more complete, resulting in increased CO2 production. Similarly, in a 94 Ah NMC cell (Huang et al., 2022) it was observed that the total production of combustible gases (including CO, CH4, C2H4, C3H6) decreased with higher heating power. In addition, Goupil et al. (2022) investigated an 18650 NMC 2.5 Ah cell under constant heating rate in a cone calorimeter and found that the total amount of smoke produced was the lowest for cells subjected to a moderate heating rate (10 ℃/min), although the specific gas composition was not discussed by the author. Note that the experiments in previous articles were conducted under an open hood, where more O2 is available to convert CO into CO2. In Table 7, the vol% of gases for LFP cells revealed a higher vol% of H2, and a lower vol% of CO2 as the heating rate increased from 12 °C/min to 24 °C/min. Interestingly, similar results were observed for a heating rate of 24 °C/min along with a constant temperature of 250 °C. Additionally, the vol% of CH4 showed a slight increase with faster heating rates, likely due to the presence of more H2, which may contribute to reduction of electrolyte to lithium carbonate and formation of CH4. CO also increased slightly. When LFP cells were heated at a rate of 12 °C/min, they emitted significantly more CO2 than those heated at a rate of 24 °C/min. Slower heating rates led to a more complete combustion of LFP. This is most likely due to increased access to oxygen facilitated through extended time for decomposition of the components. According to the authors’ knowledge, there is no available data in literature on how heating rates impact gas composition in LFP cells, making result comparisons difficult. In our experiment, the chamber temperatures for NMC cells are approx. 300–400 °C, whereas the chamber temperatures for LFP cells remain below 100 °C. The gas composition of H2 and CO2 in LFP cells appeared to be influenced by heating rates, whereas no dependency on heating rates was observed in NMC cells. However, it is unclear whether this difference is solely attributed to cell chemistry or if cell format (prismatic vs pouch) also plays a role. Further investigation is needed, such as testing cells with similar capacity and format under identical heating rates, to better understand these effects.

For different cell-types, a noticeable difference existed in the total gas volume and the cumulative gas composition. The total gas volume normalized by Ah generated by NMC cells was approximately twice that of LFP cells during the final stages. A plausible explanation for the larger gas volume in NMC cells is that the active cathode releases oxygen during decomposition. Additionally, NMC cells burn at a higher temperature compared to LFP, thus accelerating decomposition processes and allowing a higher degree of materials decomposition. Since the experiments were conducted in an inert atmosphere (with O2 <1 %), the oxygen release by the cell may contribute to cell combustion. It is feasible to consider that CO2 and CO were generated by NMC cells as a result of oxidation or partial oxidation of carbon-containing materials at both the cathode and anode. Meanwhile, the LFP crystalline structure has more stable bonds and is resistant to oxygen release (Golubkov et al., 2014). Consequently, the combustion reactions involving the electrolyte and the graphite anode, resulting in the formation of CO and CO2, depend on the availability of oxygen releasing species in the electrolyte itself. This dependence occurs particularly when there is no external supply of oxygen available for the reactions (Sturk et al., 2019). Our study does not analyze the gas release rate per cell, but the total gas release per Ah for the NMC cells is twice that of the LFP cells, consistent with findings by Sturk et al (Sturk et al., 2019). In total, NMC cells do indeed emit more CO2 per Ah than LFP cells, and LFP cells produce a lower vol% of CO per total gas release compared to NMC cells. Alternative experiments conducted in closed configurations with fully charged cells revealed that LFP cells produced less CO than NMC and NMC/LCO cells (Golubkov et al., 2014). Although CO levels in the LFP cells were not measured due to an error in the work of Sturk et al., they also found that NMC/LMO cells produced more CO2 than LFP cells (Sturk et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total heat released to pressure vessel during cell thermal runaway
ce:section.ce:para: This paper presents an alternative method for estimating the THR during cell gas release, which does not require the consideration of battery chemistry (See section 3.2.2). The estimated THR per test is according to Eq. 4, and the results are tabulated in Table 10. Table 10 shows for all pouch cell tests; the peak temperature within the chamber, the peak pressure within the chamber, the total moles of battery vent gas, the total moles of gas vented after TR and THR within the chamber. The THR data from the prismatic cells is not included in the table but is presented and discussed herewith.

Test 1 pouch cells produced 717 kJ, while Test 2 pouch cells generated 813 kJ. The higher energy output in Test 2 is due to the elevated peak temperature (368 °C) and peak pressure (387 kPa) compared to Test 1 (295 °C and 318 kPa). Tests 3 and 4 showed consistent energy outputs of 939 kJ and 937 kJ, respectively. For prismatic cells, peak temperatures and pressures were 100 °C and around 180 kPa, resulting in lower THR values of 79 kJ, 53 kJ, and 40 kJ for Tests 5, 6, and 7, respectively. According to Willstrand et al (Willstrand et al., 2024)., internal heat generation (IHG) in LIBs is proportional to the heat released from exothermic reactions. However, the internal heat from exothermic reactions cannot be directly compared to the heat absorbed by the gas environment in the pressure vessel during TR.

Literature indicates that several NMC cells have an energy output of around 10 kJ/Wh, while LFP are range from 2 – 5 kJ/Wh (Rappsilber et al., 2023). The kJ/Wh values for NMC cells in the literature are 3 times higher than those suggested by the THR method. Conversely, the kJ/Wh values for LFP cells in the literature are significantly higher, ranging from 15 to 30 times the THR estimates here. Consequently, the THR values for prismatic cells are deemed unreliable, and the presented THR method is not a suitable approach for the tested prismatic cells. Further research is required to gain insight into the extent to which the battery chemistry constrained the estimation of THR within a pressure vessel. It is noteworthy that a correlation exists between heating rates and THR. The THR of pouch cells demonstrates a direct correlation with the heating rate, exhibiting an increase in line with the rise in heating rate. Additionally, Gagnon et al. (2023) observed a corresponding increase in total chemical heat release with an elevated cell heating rate, a finding that aligns also with that of Wang et al (Wang et al., 2019b).

This study represents the initial phase of a broader experimental investigation, with its scope shaped by constraints in sample availability and available resources. Due to budget limitations, the number of test repetitions was restricted. Specifically, two parallel tests were conducted for the pouch cells, while prismatic cells were tested once per heating rate. The experimental procedures were rigorously controlled, and the results were critically compared with data from existing literature to ensure consistency, reliability, and scientific validity. The presented data provide a valuable reference point for future experimental and modelling work involving large cell formats.

This work presents the effect of different heating rates on thermal behaviour and gas compositions for two types of large cell formats, a 64 Ah NMC pouch cell and a 100 Ah LFP prismatic cell. The investigation was carried out in a pressurised vessel and focused on key safety-relevant parameters, including surface temperature evolution, thermal runaway propagation time across the cell surfaces, total mass loss, gas composition and volume, and total heat release. Conclusions are as follow:

ce:para.ce:bold: (1) Effect of heating rate on TR onset time, TR onset temperature and maximum temperature

ce:section.ce:para: The initiation time for TR decreases with increasing heating rate for both cells. In NMC pouch cells, higher heating rates also result in a lower TR onset temperature. However, LFP prismatic cells exhibited a stable plateau at 300 °C at heating rates of 12 °C/min and 24 °C/min. The relationship between heating rate and maximum temperature remains inconclusive due to sample size limitations and variability in observed trends. Some studies suggest a dependency, while others do not, indicating that factors such as cell venting dynamics during TR may influence heat dissipation and peak temperatures. Further investigation is needed to clarify these effects.

The temperatures on the horizontal surfaces of the cell are not uniformly distributed, resulting in a noticeable propagation time or delay during TR. The non-uniformity leads to considerable temperature gradients within the cell throughout preheating, TR and post-TR phases. The findings highlight the complexity of thermal transport within large cell formats and suggest that internal thermal gradients must be considered in safety assessments. With the current commercial trend of increasing cell size (above 300 Ah), particularly for stationary energy storage, this becomes even more important.

ce:para.ce:bold: (3) Effect of heating rate on TR propagation time

ce:section.ce:para: In NMC pouch cells, the time required for TR to propagate from the heater-exposed surface to the opposite surface increases with heating rate. However, the propagation time across the cell thickness shows inconsistent trends, likely influenced by factors such as internal cell structure, venting dynamics, and localized temperature variations. LFP prismatic cells displayed significant variability, preventing clear conclusions. Literature suggests that higher heating rates generally prolong TR propagation through the cell thickness, further research is necessary to confirm this trend under varying conditions.

The total gas volume released increased by approximately 0.1 L/Ah across all heating rates for both cell types. Heating rate had minimal impact on gas composition in NMC pouch cells, while in LFP cells, higher heating rates resulted in lower CO₂ and higher H₂ concentrations. This suggests that at lower heating rates, combustion is more complete in an inert atmosphere. In contrast, NMC pouch cells appear to sustain combustion through their internal oxygen supply, making heating rate less influential.

ce:para.ce:bold: (5) Effect of cell chemistry on the gas composition

ce:section.ce:para:
The composition of the cell chemistry and the cell capacity significantly influence the gas composition. LFP prismatic cells produced about 10 times less CO and
2
/
5
less CO₂ than NMC pouch cells. Both cell-types generated similar hydrocarbon levels, around 0.15 L/Ah. However, NMC pouch cells released 1.6 times more H₂ than the LFP prismatic cells.

These conclusions provide key information for improving cell design safety. It is crucial to consider that low heating rates can initiate the thermal runaway process through the venting of combustible gases and, therefore, should not be underestimated. The cell-level test method presented here serves as a foundation for early-stage fire safety evaluations in battery room design. In real applications, ignition sources such as internal short circuits, electrical sparks, inadequate thermal insulation, or poor heat dissipation can provide sufficient heat to initiate thermal runaway. Expanding the experimental matrix with additional repetitions and a broader range of heating rates would improve the robustness of the conclusions. Future research could evaluate these effects in complete modules and optimize TR initiation methods, considering the intensity and position of heating in configurations more representative of real systems.

Christoph Meraner: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - reviewing and editing, Supervision.
